'''Weller Hauraki''' (born 18 February 1985) is a New Zealand former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played as a {{rlp|sr}} or {{rlp|lf}} and played for the [[New Zealand Māori rugby league team|New Zealand Māori]] at international level.

He has played for the [[Parramatta Eels]] in the [[NRL]], and [[Crusaders Rugby League|Crusaders RL]], [[Leeds Rhinos]], [[Castleford Tigers]], [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] and the [[Salford Red Devils]] in the [[Super League]], spending time on loan from [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]] at the [[Widnes Vikings]] in 2018.

==Background==
Hauraki was born in [[Dannevirke]], New Zealand.

===Early career===

==Manawatu (2002)==
[[File:Weller Hauraki 2008.jpg|thumb|right|Weller Hauraki in action]]

===2002===
Hauraki played for [[Manawatu rugby league team|Manawatu]] in the [[2002 New Zealand rugby league season|2002 North Island Super 6 competition]].<ref name=manawatu>[http://www.rleague.com/db/article.php?id=13524 Wellington and Manawatu Teams] ''rleague.com'', 20 September 2002</ref>

===Senior career===

==Parramatta Eels (2007-09)==
[[File:Weller Hauraki.jpg|thumb|right|Hauraki playing in 2009]]

===2007-09===
Hauraki began his career in the [[National Rugby League]] playing for the [[Parramatta Eels]], he made his début against the [[New Zealand Warriors]] during the 2007 season, and he went on to make 32 appearances over three seasons. Hauraki also won the 2007 [[Intrust Super Premiership NSW]] competition with [[Parramatta Eels|Parramatta]], scoring a try right on the final siren to defeat the [[North Sydney Bears]], 20-15.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.abc.net.au/news/2007-09-30/peachey-falls-short-in-farewell-appearance/2681812|title = Peachey falls short in farewell appearance|website = [[Australian Broadcasting Corporation]]|date = 30 September 2007}}</ref>
His most memorable game for the [[Parramatta Eels|Eels]] came in round 6 of the 2009 season, when he scored two tries against the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]], after replacing his injured teammate [[Nathan Hindmarsh]].

==Crusaders RL (2010)==
===2010===
In 2010, to ease pressure on the [[Parramatta Eels]]' salary cap, Hauraki was released<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/weller-hauraki-release-first-step-in-cap-cleanout/story-e6frfgbo-1225820581735 |title=Weller Hauraki release first step in cap cleanout |newspaper=Herald Sun |date=18 January 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110614042338/http://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/weller-hauraki-release-first-step-in-cap-cleanout/story-e6frfgbo-1225820581735 |archive-date=14 June 2011}}</ref> and went on to sign a two-year contract with the [[Crusaders Rugby League|Crusaders RL]] club in the [[Super League]].{{citation needed|date=January 2020}}

He made his début for the [[Crusaders Rugby League|Crusaders]] against the [[Wigan Warriors]] on 5 February 2010, at the [[DW Stadium]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=16453 |title=Noble looking for new signings |website=Super League |date=30 January 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100201005227/http://www.superleague.co.uk/article.php?id=16453 |archive-date=1 February 2010}}</ref>

==Leeds Rhinos (2011–12)==
===2011===
After his very impressive first season in the [[Super League]], Hauraki signed a three-year contract to play for the [[Leeds Rhinos]].

He made his début from off the interchange bench against [[Hull F.C.]] and he scored in the [[Leeds Rhinos|Rhinos]]' 32-18 victory.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/haurakis-charge-for-rhinos-halted-2194349.html|location=London|work=The Independent|first=Dave|last=Hadfield|title=Hauraki's charge for Rhinos halted|date=26 January 2011}}</ref>

He played at {{rlp|lf}} for [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] in the [[2011 Challenge Cup Final]] defeat by the [[Wigan Warriors]] at [[Wembley Stadium]].<ref>{{cite news|last=wiganwarriors.com|title=Wigan are 2011 Cup Champions|url=http://www.rleague.com/content/article.php?id=40914|access-date=21 November 2019|newspaper=rleague.com|date=28 August 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last=Scott|first=Ged|title=Leeds 18-28 Wigan|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/14670204|access-date=21 November 2019|newspaper=[[BBC Sport]]|date=27 August 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last=Henson|first=Mike|title=Challenge Cup final - as it happened|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/9574171.stm|access-date=21 November 2019|newspaper=[[BBC Sport]]|date=27 August 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last=Brown|first=Oliver|title=Challenge Cup final 2011: Wigan's warrior spirit shades controversy in emphatic win over Leeds Rhinos|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/8728278/Challenge-Cup-final-2011-Wigans-warrior-spirit-shades-controversy-in-emphatic-win-over-Leeds-Rhinos.html|access-date=21 November 2019|newspaper=The Telegraph|date=28 August 2011}}</ref>

==Castleford Tigers (2013–14)==
===2013===
Hauraki then joined the [[Castleford Tigers]] ahead of the 2013 season.

Weller quickly established himself in what surprisingly became one of the [[Super League]]'s most feared packs, helping the [[Castleford Tigers]] cement their highest-ever [[Super League]] finish of 4th.{{citation needed|date=October 2022}}

===2014===
He was also instrumental in a run to the [[2014 Challenge Cup Final]] defeat by the [[Leeds Rhinos]] at [[Wembley Stadium]],<ref name="Leeds lift Challenge Cup after Ryan Hall’s double stuns Castleford">{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2014/aug/23/catleford-leeds-challenge-cup-final-match-report|title=Leeds lift Challenge Cup after Ryan Hall's double stuns Castleford|publisher=Guardian|date=23 August 2014|access-date=13 November 2019}}</ref> with that ending in a 23-10 defeat by his former club and rivals the [[Leeds Rhinos]].

==Salford Red Devils (2015–18)==
===2015-18===
Hauraki represented the [[Salford Red Devils]] for four consecutive seasons, recording 73 appearances and scoring 14 tries for the club in total.{{citation needed|date=October 2022}}

==Widnes Vikings (2018)==
===2018===
During the 2018 season, Hauraki was sent out on loan from the [[Salford Red Devils]] to the struggling [[Widnes Vikings]], where he subsequently suffered relegation from the [[Super League]] with the [[Widnes Vikings|Vikings]] at the end of the campaign.{{citation needed|date=October 2022}}

==Hull Kingston Rovers (2019–2020)==
===2019===
It was revealed on 3 October 2018, that Hauraki had committed to a two-year deal to join [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] ahead of the 2019 [[Super League]] season.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/45729933|title=Jimmy Keinhorst, Weller Hauraki & Ryan Lannon: Hull Kingston Rovers make triple signing|work=BBC Sport|date=3 October 2018|access-date=25 October 2018}}</ref>

Hauraki made his [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] début on 1 February 2019, in round 1 of the [[Super League]] season against cross-city rivals [[Hull F.C.]] He started the game at {{rlp|lf}} and went on to record a thrilling 18-16 victory at [[Craven Park, Hull|Craven Park]].

Weller scored his first try for [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] on 23 February 2019, in a 22-24 [[Super League]] round 4 defeat by the [[Salford Red Devils]].

===2020===
Hauraki was released by Hull KR at the end of the 2020 season.

===2021===
On 9 February 2021 it was reported that Hauraki had decided to retire.<ref>{{cite web|website=BBC Sport|date=9 Feb 2021|access-date=11 Feb 2021|title=Weller Hauraki: Ex-Hull KR and Salford Red Devils forward retires|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/55993566}}</ref>

==Representative career (2005–10)==
Hauraki was named in the [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] training squad for the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]], but did not make the final squad.<ref name="Kiwis select Sonny Bill">{{cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12196_3949034,00.html|title=Kiwis select Sonny Bill|publisher=Sky Sports|date=2008-08-08|access-date=2008-08-08}}</ref>

However, Hauraki did play for the [[New Zealand Māori rugby league team|New Zealand Māori]] in their 34-26 defeat by the [[Indigenous All Stars (Rugby League)|Indigenous All Stars]] before the start of the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]], having previously represented the side in 2005.<ref>{{cite book
|last =John Coffey, Bernie Wood
|title = 100 years: Māori rugby league, 1908-2008
|publisher =Huia Publishers
|year = 2008
|pages = 332
|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=nklWo8vw-iIC
|isbn =9781869693312}}</ref>

Hauraki again played for the [[New Zealand Māori rugby league team|New Zealand Māori]] in 2010, in the 18-18 draw against [[England national rugby league team|England]].<ref name="NZ_Herald_10681035">{{cite news|url=http://www.nzherald.co.nz/sport/news/article.cfm?c_id=4&objectid=10681035&ref=rss|title=Maori draw with England|date=16 October 2010|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]]|access-date=2 December 2011}}</ref>
